brief:
The single most striking feature of this week's curve is not what moved but what didn't — **zero basis points of change across every tenor**, from the 91-day bill at **9.85%** to the 15-year bond at **12.68%**. A perfectly frozen yield curve in early April, with fiscal year-end looming in June, tells us something important: the market has reached a temporary equilibrium, but it is an equilibrium born of caution, not conviction. The front end is anchored just below the **10% repo rate**, with the 91-day bill at **9.85%** and call money trading at **9.80%**, both hugging the policy floor as if daring Bangladesh Bank to blink. The 10Y-91D spread sits at **130 basis points**, which sounds reasonable until you notice the glaring distortions in the belly — the 2-year at **11.95%** is trading **80 bps above** the 5-year at **10.82%** and **80 bps above** the 10-year at **11.15%**, creating an unmistakable kink that screams supply anxiety in the medium term. The 15-year at **12.68%** towers over the 20-year at **11.65%** by over a full percentage point, another inversion that signals fragmented demand rather than any coherent view on terminal rates. Auction dynamics reinforce this picture: T-bills cleared cleanly, with the 91-day attracting a healthy bid-cover of **1.92x** and zero devolvement, but the 10-year BGTB managed only **1.4x** coverage and suffered **10% devolvement** on March 8 — a clear signal that the market is telling the government it wants more compensation for duration risk but the government isn't willing to pay it.

The structural backdrop explains why the curve looks this way. Excess liquidity at **৳1.9 lakh crore** is enormous by any historical standard, yet it is sitting in the banking system like water behind a dam, unable to find productive outlets. The advance-to-deposit ratio at **75.8%** confirms that credit demand remains tepid — banks are flush with deposits they cannot lend. M2 growth at **9.5%** is running below headline CPI of **9.13%**, meaning real money supply growth is essentially flat, which constrains any meaningful expansion in private credit. On the fiscal side, the numbers are sobering: revenue collection at **44.9% of target** with three months left in FY26 means the government faces a widening financing gap that will inevitably translate into heavier T-bill and bond issuance in Q4. ADP implementation at a dismal **11.5%** suggests development spending will be back-loaded, adding to government borrowing pressure precisely when the market is least prepared for it. The interest-to-revenue ratio at **22%** is a flashing amber light — every fifth taka of government revenue already goes to debt service, and any upward repricing of the curve materially worsens this dynamic. Meanwhile, the exchange rate at **৳121.2** against the dollar and reserves at **$35.1 billion** provide a fragile stability; Brent at **$78.4** and JKM LNG at **$12.8/mmbtu** keep the energy import bill manageable for now, but a spike in either would pressure reserves and force BB's hand on tightening, something the curve is not pricing in at all.

For positioning this week, I see one clear opportunity and two clear risks. The opportunity is in the **5-year tenor at 10.82%** — it cleared at **1.6x** bid-cover with zero devolvement, meaning the market accepted this level willingly, and it sits **113 bps above** the 2-year on a maturity-adjusted basis once you account for the 2-year's anomalous **11.95%** print. If you believe BB holds the repo rate at **10%** through the rest of FY26 — and the frozen front end suggests the market does — then the 5-year offers genuine carry at **82 bps** over repo with manageable duration. The first risk is the **10-year and beyond**, where devolvement is already appearing and the curve inversions between 15-year and 20-year signal that primary dealers are warehousing paper they cannot distribute. I would avoid adding duration past 7 years until we see bid-cover ratios improve above **1.5x** consistently. The second risk is the fiscal fourth quarter itself: with revenue running **5+ percentage points** behind a straight-line target, expect the government to lean heavily on T-bill issuance, which could push the 91-day above the **10% repo** and destabilize the front end. The catalyst to watch is the next 10-year auction — if devolvement rises above **10%** or bid-cover falls below **1.3x**, the curve will need to reprice higher by **15-25 bps** across the belly, and we should be positioned to buy that dip rather than be caught holding overvalued duration into it.
